message:
Black is a problematic word, but I don't necessarily think there's a better one.  It suggests a kind of binary opposition -- black and white being literal opposites chromatically speaking -- but comes pre-associated with ideas of other binary oppositions, namely good and evil.  

Caucasian people **aren't** white.  African peoples **aren't** black. Suggesting that they are -- or even more problematically, suggesting that the various shades and tones between Irish and Congolese are enumerable as "White" and "Black" -- just re-enforces that dichotomy and serves to place undue emphasis on a superficial distinction.  

To be clear here, I'm not in any way saying I have a better solution.  You could as easily say "my cappuccino friend, Miles" or "my teak friend, Charles" but I'm quite aware that this is never going to catch on and that no one wants to spend 10 minutes thinking of a marketable Pantone shade for each of their friends.

Black and white aren't "perfectly fine" ways to talk about race but they're probably the best we've got right now.  If language is about making yourself understood rather than trying to change what people think (which is a bit Orwellian for my tastes, even if it's well intentioned) then I'd be hard pressed to come up with an alternative, but that doesn't mean that we shouldn't be aware of the baggage and shortcomings inherent in the words we use.

I appreciate the thoughtful consideration of terminology, @slow-university. However, I believe we should strive for progress, not just acknowledge the status quo. While "black" and "white" might be entrenched, finding alternatives could help shift perceptions. Maybe we can start small, like in professional settings or educational materials, and gradually build from there.

message: I'm not sure why we keep having to explain this. Objectification isn't just about women; it's about how people are treated, period. And yes, feminism does focus on women because that's who it was created to help. But that doesn't mean we can't look at the bigger picture too. Let's not pretend that every issue has to be about men and women equally to be valid.

I get tired of these same old arguments. Objectification isn't just about women, but it's also not just about men. It's about how society treats individuals, regardless of gender. And feminism isn't just about women; it's about equality. We need to address all forms of discrimination, not just focus on one group. Let's move past these oversimplified views and work towards real change.

There's a multitude of reasons why, in the current culture, it's more difficult to create a RomCom type movie featuring minority leads. I want to clarify that from a fairness way of looking at it, this is absolutely terrible and while it looks like it's getting better, we're still far off from having interracial and minority relationships being the front and center of RomComs. 


Currently, American RomComs are mostly a white-women's genre. It's fair to say that this is mostly because the characters and casting choices are white. This creates an interesting dilemma; most RomCom moviegoers are white, so it makes sense to cast white actors. This makes it logical to not write RomComs for minority characters because most RomCom moviegoers are white so the characters should be white etc etc etc rinse and repeat. 

So the next step would be some major studio that has the power and advertising budget to create a RomCom blockbuster with minority leads that won't be pigeonholed for a minority audience. That's easier said than done in today's current social climate. Not only from a business perspective is it unwise for a major movie studio to invest in a minority lead film  (financially speaking of course. Legacy wise, this would be huge for whatever movie studio manages to pull this off), but you also have to navigate the social divisions to make the film palatable to the most moviegoers possible. 

The pigeonholing of minority led movies to being "for minorities" is a huge problem with making RomComs with minority leads. Some minorities are offended if the minority characters don't act like their race. Some don't like it if the writers and directors of the minority characters are not in the same minority group. Don't get me wrong, the wanting of correct representation is completely valid and understandble, but that sort of attention to detail with minority characters isn't appreciated in RomComs or any slice of life movies (currently). 

Most RomCom characters don't have a cultural identity. There's nothing about the characters that specifically speaks to a race or ethnicity, but they are mostly cast with white actors in the past because of racism, and now because to properly depict the minority group would alienate (not necessarily offend but remove the ability to connect) for the population majority white audience. It's not fair that minorities are to watch 90% of films being made today that don't have characters that they can culturally connect to, but as the majority of the US population is white, it's makes sense that the movie studios continue to make these movies. 

There are some RomComs that are made with minority leads that aren't related to the race of the main cheaters at all. The Last Holiday with Queen Latifah comes to mind. I really enjoy that movie but even then, the box office was still under budget.  

Despite all this, don't get too in despair-mode. Things are changing, fast. Some of the biggest shows on ABC (which is now like freeform or something, I don't even know anymore) are created by Shonda Rhimes, a black woman who has created a dynasty on great shows that feature minority actors. One of the new Star Wars main characters is a minority. Orange is the New Black is heavily focused on minority characters. Other shows on Netlix like The Get Down and Sense8 also feature Minorty actors. Even the Lifetime channel, which is basically catnip for middle aged white women, had a black man as the romantic lead for UnREAL season 2. Get Out, a satire on race relations, has been heralded as one of the best horror movies in this decade. 

We're still far, far off from having racially blind casting in the majority of film media, but things are getting better. We're still several years off from having major movie studios providing adequate funding for minority led slice of life films.

message:
I don't think the issue is that people want to randomly replace characters with women or minorities. The issue is that this is somehow the default and stories of minorities aren't told. I'm an Asian woman and no one in this conversation has mentioned Asian people. The only stories of Asian people told are focused on some great character. You don't get an everyday story like You've Got Mail focused on Asian people or an interracial couple. 

Whitewashing Asian roles has been a big problem in Hollywood, too. You say we shouldn't randomly replace characters with women or minorities, but producers are casting Asian roles with white people. See: The Last Airbender, Aloha (Emma Stone as Allison Ng), Doctor Strange (Tilda Swinton as a Tibetan monk), Ghost in the Shell (Scarlett Johansson as Motoko Kusanagi). They always have some rationalization for why they need white people, like white people sell better, but how are Asian actors going to become famous and sell better if they're not given lead roles. 

Even when Asians are cast, they're usually the only Asian in the cast. You don't get to see relationships between two Asian people featured the way you get to see many white or black relationships. 

I feel like people like me aren't given the same opportunities in the film industry. If I saw Asian women in media all the time, sure, I could feel like I had the luxury to identify with whoever I wanted, but right now I just feel underrepresented in media. Who I am includes being Asian, being the daughter of immigrants, growing up with Chinese culture.

@another-body I hear you, but let's not forget that progress takes time. And sometimes, even when there are Asian leads, the story might not revolve around their cultural background. Like in *The Joy Luck Club*, the focus wasn't on the cultural aspects but on the personal journeys of the characters. It's a step in the right direction, even if it's not perfect.

It's an inherently unjust act because they're our kindred. I would think so the same of a stray cat attacking a domestic cat, or a crocodile eating the offspring of another. It is an inherently unjust act to commit unjustified acts of violence against the same species as yours. I did not try to justify my bias, however, I even acknowledged it, I presented it for all to see and try to change my mind on. Why is it unfair to think of your species' needs above the well-being of other species?

I doesn't feel like you ever present an actual argument for why animals are insignificant. You kind of assume it, maybe because you have a large bias for humans. You seem to hold the position that harming humans is an inherently unjust act and to do so would need justification, but harming an animal doesn't need any such justification. If so, why? What trait do animals lack that justifies this difference in treatment that if were absent in humans would justify the same treatment to humans? 

The reason you won't be able to find a trait is because your argument is based on the premise that animals have no value, with no actual justification behind said premise. You state they are less rational, which is true, but there are plenty of humans that are irrational as well. Does that mean that killing or abusing those humans doesn't need justification? Or do you just discriminate against animals on the sole basis that they aren't humans, and try to mask that with weak "justifications" such as that they are less rational, or dumber?

@jellied-bias I guess you'd say that the crocodile has no stake in the outcome of the domestic cat's life, but the human does have a stake in the outcome of another human's life. So, by that logic, we should prioritize our own species over others, right? Just saying, if we start down this path, where does it end? With slavery? Eugenics? Because, let's face it, those weren't exactly popular among certain groups until recently.

I don't think comparing a crocodile to a human is fair. Humans have complex emotions and consciousness, while animals don't. That's a big difference. And yes, I do believe in treating humans with more consideration, but that doesn't mean we should ignore other species entirely. We can still protect animals while also prioritizing our own kind.

@jellied-bias Crocodiles might not care about cats, but humans often don't care about each other either. So, maybe we should start caring more about everyone, not just ourselves. @jellied-bias I get what you're saying, but it feels like you're trying to make a point about priorities that's a bit too extreme. Sure, humans have complex emotions and consciousness, but so do many other animals. And just because we can reason doesn't mean we should ignore the suffering of others, whether they're human or not. @sharp-trumpet I hear you, but let's not get carried away. Compassion is great, but sometimes we need to draw lines. Sure, animals feel pain, but they don't understand the concept of empathy or morality like humans do. So, while I agree we should protect them, shouldn't we focus on helping humans first since they can actually change their ways? Plus, isn't it a bit hypocritical to demand compassion from humans when we often fail to show it to each other?

Well, there's the difference that we are humans, so we shouldn't kill our fellow brethren (for a variety of reasons, including that it increases the chance of us being killed).  
  
There's also the difference that we're more intelligent than any other animal.   
  
The issue here, I think, is sentience.  If something is sentient it's wrong to kill it, if not, well it's just a bug.  The problem is that sentience isn't binary, it's linear, so while a moose isn't as intelligent as a human being, it's still sentient to some degree I think.  Vs a bug which is likely not at all.  
  
Killing animals should be done when there's a good reason, and is fully justified when there is.  I think anyways.

The boss wasn't, he was a normal part of the game. All we saw of late game was V ganking earlygame content with the lategame claws. I'm not saying the trailer should *make us* assume the combat gets good later, but it's definitely fair to assume the combat will have thought involved. The boss looked to be more s tutorial for weak spots than anything, if the game develops in that direction significantly the combat could be very good.

The auto-aim guns seem to be a tradeoff with other benefits, giving people who don't want to aim options whilst still rewarding aiming by means of stronger alternatives. Seems fair for an RPG.

As far as the actual CMV goes- whilst you might not do the same, could you not admit that, assuming you're optimistic and give the gameplay a bit of benefit of the doubt, the game is 'deserving' of hype as a result?

The game isn't a shooter, it's am RPG. You're placing undue emphasis on the importance of the shooting overall. The stealth looked like it had potential to expand in more interesting directions, and the driving looked serviceable at worst.

I think the biggest reason it deserves the hype is the city it presents. It's, by far, the closest a game has ever come to portraying an actual city centre, the street-crossing portion is simply staggering compared to any other game's cityscape. The game also showed superb visuals, good role playing options with quests thag have several dynamic paths, and a downright  impressive conversation system.

On the balance, the gameplay looks super fucking good as a whole and deserves hype.

There is going to be *a* fast food place and *a* gas station. There is no way to assure that either will be hiring. So what should they do if they aren't? And doing what you are saying would more likely end up with a shotgun pointed at you than a job.

Mostly, it is because we're Americans.  We give assistance under the general assumption that *"I know you'd rather be self sufficient, but here's something until then to get back you on your feet."* and we give people a choice of what they eat as our way of saying *"I don't know if your family prefers spaghetti/borscht/mashed-potatoes/matzo/hummus/rice/tacos; so here's some cash to get you whatever it is you need."*

If someone on EBT/FoodStamps decides to eat cup ramen 6-days-a-week and then splurge for a small steak on day-7, is that really such a grave sin?  Saying *"No, that is your choice"* means that as long as they're living within their means (even if some/all of those means are gifted) we are fine with their freedom to make such a decision; saying *"Yes, that is a problem; they can't eat that on my paycheck!"* implies that we're more preoccupied with them not-enjoying their meals than we are with them living within their means.  Granted, someone eating lobster and filet mignon daily while we taxpayers are eating fishsticks and hamburger would be a slap to the face, but to what extent is that really happening?

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Points 1, 3, 4, 5, 7, and even 6 are almost all the same as far as the above goes.  You're deciding that *"Beggars can't be choosers; and this is the meal that I want you to dislike today."* is the optimal path to self-sufficiency.  Why even limit EBT/FoodStamps to only vegan items, when we could force benefit-receivers to subsist off of State-provided vegan-sourced crackers and gruel instead?  While that statement is purposefully a bit *reductio ad absurdum* I believe the point still stands.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As for Point 2, limiting food-stamp applicability is going to do little for the expense and availability of healthier foods.  Even if it is assumed that limitation of EBT/FoodStamps will cut into a large percentage of urban corner store food purchases, that in no way means that that current food deserts are going to become local grocers to recoup those fractional losses.  The supply chain and logistics of easily-perishable foods is a rather large hurdle.

The other issue is that the above ignores the difference between ready-to-eat foods and cheap vegan ingredients.  Cheap processed food is often designed to be ready-to-eat.  Even if vegan products were somehow available at comparable costs, convenience (in not having to cook/prepare something) will often demand a premium.

Plus, forcing them to eat only certain foods might just teach them to appreciate a variety of options.

message:
I've been browsing the top posts for a while and I don't think I've see a men are biologically inferior narrative. Men are the main perpetrators of sexual violence, so the dogma that stems from that information is based in reality. Not all men are rapists, but most rapists are men, yet rape is seen as a women's issue when men are the cause of it. This can subconsciously put the burden to fix rape on women, as it's their issue. 

When men step up and accept that they may be the cause of these problems, /r/gendercritical is positive about it, [Take this post](https://www.reddit.com/r/GenderCritical/comments/8do8gb/terry_crews_men_dont_see_women_as_all_the_way/) as an example.

But the fact that it's based in reality doesn't make it any less hurtful towards men, like systemic racism is sometimes based in reality when it comes to crime, but that doesn't mean those forms of racism are any more okay than the other ones.

@augmenting-canal I get where you're coming from, but framing it like that can really hurt a lot of good men who aren't rapists. It's important to address the issue without making everyone feel like they're part of the problem. And @greasy-element, systemic issues are complex, but dismissing the reality of the statistics just because they're uncomfortable isn't helpful either. Let's try to find a balanced approach.

When discussing sociological ideas, it is appropriate to use sociological meanings of words.  The word privilege is related to power, and power dynamics.  My definition for it is "greater access to power and agency for one class over another" (keep in mind that definitions don't demonstrate meaning, it's just a good starting point.  That is to say, you don't understand all the nuance of what I mean when I say "privilege" simply by reading that definition, or even this whole reply, but it's a general gist of what I am getting at).  It is *not* just "advantages."

The comparison that I've found best demonstrates where people go wrong with privilege is when looking at adults and children.  To be an adult is to have responsibility.  You have to get work and if you have children, you are supposed to take care of them.  Children get to basically not care about anything.  Even the things they are supposed to be responsible about they don't *really* need to be responsible for.

So there are benefits to being a child.  However, you would never in a million years declare that it is children who have power and agency in the world.  They are often literally called "dependents" because at the end of the day, they are dependent on someone else to provide for them.

Going back to male privilege, the things you list *are (sometimes, I'm sure we would disagree on specifics if we dug into it) valid issues that men face*, but similar to how grown ups have to deal with bad things that children don't because of the power and agency that adults have, these things stem from male privilege.

To head one thing off, if you don't want to use that version of "privilege" that's fine, but since that's what people familiar with sociology and feminism mean when they say it, if you want to argue with them it's appropriate to specify whether you are arguing about the *semantics* or the *idea*.  Lots  of people like to make semantics arguments in order to argue against an idea, but that doesn't work (if you want to engage in honest debate anyways.  It works fucking great for trolls).
